title: Manage The Day (MTD) – AI Productivity Platform
summary: A cross-platform AI productivity platform — a unified multi-provider calendar, a voice + text AI planning assistant, and live iOS Dynamic Island timers.
overview: MTD is a production-grade productivity platform I work on as a software engineer at Connecting Points Tech — across the whole stack: Flutter clients for mobile, web and desktop, a FastAPI backend, and an AI planning assistant. It unifies four external calendar providers, takes natural-language commands by voice or text, and drives live iOS Dynamic Island timers — bringing a person's entire day (tasks, projects, goals and time) into one place.
details: **The problem** — People run their day across disconnected tools: tasks in one app; Google, Apple, Outlook and the phone's own calendar in others; goals and time-tracking nowhere at all. Every day starts with manually reconciling all of it.

**Unified multi-provider calendar** — the hardest, most distinctive piece. I built one calendar surface (on Syncfusion) that merges MTD's own events with Google Calendar, Apple/iCloud via CalDAV, Microsoft 365 via Graph and on-device iOS via EventKit — each with different auth, token-refresh and sync semantics — into a single colour-coded view with drag-and-drop, resize, recurrence and timezone-correct scheduling.

**Two-way sync & conflict handling** — edits in MTD write back to the source provider and vice-versa; I handled token refresh, pagination, deleted/moved events and per-provider quirks so the merged view stays consistent.

**Live iOS activities** — integrated ActivityKit / Live Activities so an active focus timer shows live on the Dynamic Island and lock screen, mirrored by a native Android foreground service — the current task stays visible without opening the app.

**Voice + text AI assistant** — an in-app assistant (FastAPI + Google Agent Development Kit, Genkit and Firebase Functions) that takes natural language by text or voice and manages tasks, events, goals and pillars — and auto-schedules work onto the calendar through tool-calling.

**Full productivity system** — Task Cloud (kanban with subtasks and drag-to-schedule), Pillars & Goals for life-area planning, Projects, Focus Time / time-boxing, Time Entries, and a Reporting view with per-pillar analytics (tracked vs target, efficiency, active days).

**Architecture** — one Flutter (Dart 3) codebase for iOS, Android, Web and Desktop with layered state: BLoC for long-lived global state, Provider for UI-reactive state (timers, connectivity) and Riverpod for MCP tokens and audit logs; Firebase for Auth, Firestore real-time sync, Storage and Analytics; a FastAPI backend over Dio.

**Monetization** — one **cross-platform** subscription layer with **RevenueCat**: in-app purchases on mobile and **Stripe** on web & desktop, with Individual & Team plans, entitlement checks and purchase restore.
**Tested** — a broad automated test suite (unit, widget and Mockito mock tests) spanning auth, billing, calendars, scheduling, notifications and more.

**My role** — I work across the stack on MTD: the Flutter clients (mobile, web and desktop), the FastAPI + Google ADK backend and AI assistant, and the platform-specific hard problems (cross-provider calendar sync, Live Activities, real-time data flow) — from design to a shipped product.

title: Crooze – Fitness Challenge App
summary: A community fitness-challenge app — cycling and walking contests, leaderboards, and automated certificate generation.
overview: Crooze is a community-driven fitness app (iOS & Android) where users join admin-hosted cycling and walking challenges to earn rewards and certificates. As a software engineer at The Internet Folks I shipped three challenge modules (Community, Club, Individual) plus leaderboards and automated certificates, then hardened a live product — cutting GraphQL over-fetching and resolving crash loops and Android Binder transaction failures.
details: **Context** — Crooze is a community fitness app where admins host cycling and walking challenges and users compete for rewards and certificates; I joined to ship features and harden a live product.
**Three challenge modes** — built Community, Club and Individual challenges end to end, each with its own enrolment rules, leaderboards and challenge-summary views.
**Leaderboards & certificates** — leaderboards ranking participants by distance and points, plus automated certificate generation for challenge winners.
**GraphQL performance** — integrated and tuned GraphQL queries to cut over-fetching across challenge and profile screens (fetching only the fields each screen needs), reducing payloads and latency.

**Stability engineering** — diagnosed and fixed crash loops, null-reference crashes and state inconsistencies on a live app; a notable one was resolving **Android Binder transaction failures** by throttling isolate updates and optimising background data processing.
**Architecture** — BLoC state management on a Firebase backend, structured for a shipped consumer app across iOS and Android.
**My role** — feature development and reliability work as a software engineer at The Internet Folks.

title: AgreEasy – B2B Agriculture Platform
summary: A multilingual B2B agriculture platform where admins send crop-specific sowing requests that farmers accept or reject — plus land allocation & inspection, price negotiation, irrigation, tasks, and in-app support.
overview: AgreEasy is a multilingual B2B agriculture platform (iOS & Android) connecting farmers with buyers. Its core loop: an admin raises demand for specific crops and sends sowing requests to farmers, who accept or reject them. Around that sit land allocation and inspection, price negotiation, irrigation and task management, and in-app support with training. As a software engineer at The Internet Folks I built core farmer-facing modules end to end and hardened a live product.
details: **The problem** — farm-to-business trade runs on informal, ad-hoc deals: buyers have no structured way to signal crop demand, agree a price, or trust that land and produce are real. AgreEasy digitises the whole loop between farmers and buyers (hotels, restaurants and wholesalers).

**Sowing requests — the core loop** — an admin raises demand for a specific crop and sends a sowing request to matched farmers; each farmer reviews the crop, quantity, offered price, sowing window and location and **accepts or rejects** it, turning raw demand into committed supply.

**Land allocation & inspection** — farmers register land and allocate specific plots to crops; a land-inspection workflow verifies each plot (soil, irrigation, government-ID details) before it's approved against a request.
**Farmer onboarding** — authentication, profile management and an Add-Farm flow capturing soil type, irrigation method and government-ID fields (Tehsil / Block) with validation — built end to end.
**Price negotiation** — an in-app negotiation flow lets farmers and buyers settle on a crop price before commitment, so both sides agree terms up front.
**Irrigation & task management** — irrigation-facility tracking and farm task management keep an accepted crop on schedule through the season.
**In-app support with live chat** — farmers raise tickets for any issue and resolve them over live chat, and embedded video demos train first-time users directly inside the app.
**Multilingual for the last mile** — full localization (e.g., Hindi) so regional farmers use the entire app in their own language — essential for real adoption in rural India.

**Reliability & my role** — as a software engineer at The Internet Folks (iOS & Android) I built these farmer-facing modules end to end, optimised the ticketing system, fixed notification-delivery bugs and eliminated memory leaks — with Riverpod state management on a GraphQL + Firebase stack.

title: SimbaCourse – Education Management App
summary: A full-stack school platform replacing pen-and-paper — online attendance, real-time tracking, enrollment, fees & dues, online exams, and course management, on mobile and desktop.
overview: SimbaCourse is an education-management platform that moves schools from pen-and-paper to software. Teachers take attendance online, students track their attendance in real time, and admins handle enrollment, fees, exams and courses — from one app that runs on both mobile and desktop. Over a six-month SDE internship at SimbaQuartz I built it full-stack: a Flutter (Provider) client, an Express.js + MongoDB backend, Firebase Auth, and AWS S3 for media.
details: **From paper to software** — replaced manual pen-and-paper school workflows with a single platform serving admins, teachers and students.
**Attendance** — teachers mark attendance online per class; students see their attendance update in real time, with per-subject and overall percentages.
**Enrollment & fees** — student enrollment, fee payment and a dues tracker that surfaces what's outstanding and when.
**Academics** — online exams, course details and content, and domain & training management for the institution.
**Role-based experiences** — distinct admin, teacher and student flows over a shared data model.
**Full-stack build** — a Flutter client (Provider state management) for mobile and desktop; an Express.js + MongoDB backend; Firebase Auth for sign-in; and AWS S3 for file and media storage.
**My role** — end-to-end delivery over a six-month internship: data model, REST API and the shipped cross-platform app.

title: RepoIntel — Repository Intelligence for AI Agents
summary: An intelligence layer that builds persistent, machine-readable repository memory so AI agents skip rediscovery — exposed over MCP, ~190x smaller context than raw source.
overview: RepoIntel is an open-source Python tool I built that analyzes a codebase once and stores a persistent, machine-readable repository memory — architecture, dependencies, conventions, relationships, and change impact — so every AI agent loads understanding in seconds instead of re-exploring from scratch. It exposes that memory over an MCP server (10 tools) for any MCP-compatible agent, and compresses a repo context ~190x (about 56k tokens of source to a ~290-token context pack).
details: **The problem** — every AI coding agent re-explores a repo each session (READMEs, structure, dependencies, architecture), wasting tokens and time and reaching inconsistent understanding.

**Repository memory** — a single `repointel build` fingerprints the project, runs dependency and inventory analysis, generates an architecture graph (imports / calls / inheritance), discovers conventions, and writes a persistent `.repointel/` store.
**MCP integration** — a built-in MCP server exposes 10 tools so Claude, Codex, Gemini or any MCP agent can consume the memory directly.
**Change-impact & explanation** — predicts the blast radius before you edit a file, and explains a module's purpose, consumers and risk — with no LLM calls.

**Engineering** — Python 3.12 + uv, a multi-language plugin system (add languages without touching core), incremental updates (re-analyze only what changed), and 89 passing tests; benchmarked at ~190x context reduction.

title: PC Health Diagnostic Tool
summary: A cross-platform desktop diagnostics app — a Flutter UI over a native C++ engine via Dart FFI: system scans, live monitoring, guided hardware tests, and exportable reports.
overview: PC Health Diagnostic Tool is an open-source desktop app I built that pairs a Flutter UI with a shared native C++ diagnostics core over Dart FFI. It runs quick and full system-health scans, live performance and device monitoring, and guided hardware and system tests, and exports diagnostic reports as JSON, text, or HTML — on Windows, Linux, and macOS.
details: **Context** — diagnosing a PC's health usually means juggling several scattered utilities; PC Health brings scans, live monitoring and guided hardware tests into one clean cross-platform desktop app.
**Architecture** — a Flutter app (screens / services / models) calls a native C++ diagnostics engine through a Dart FFI bridge, with a platform layer wiring up the desktop targets.
**Diagnostics core (C++)** — modules, rules and test orchestration drive quick and full scans plus guided hardware and system tests.
**Live monitoring & reports** — real-time performance and device monitoring (CPU, memory, temperatures, disk), with exportable diagnostic reports in JSON, text and HTML.
**Cross-platform desktop** — Windows, Linux and macOS from one codebase, with incremental updates.
**My role** — sole author: the Flutter UI, the Dart FFI bridge, and the native C++ diagnostics engine.

title: Vehicle Detection & Counting
summary: A computer-vision pipeline that detects moving vehicles in traffic video and counts them as they cross a virtual line, with a live on-screen counter.
overview: A computer-vision project that detects moving vehicles in traffic footage and counts them as they cross a virtual line. Built in Python with OpenCV, it processes video frames, isolates moving objects, tracks them across frames, and increments a live on-screen counter — demonstrating classical CV and real-time video processing.
details: **Context** — counting vehicles from traffic footage by hand is slow and error-prone; automated counting is a classic computer-vision problem with real use in traffic monitoring and planning.
**Detection pipeline** — each frame runs background subtraction to isolate moving vehicles, morphological cleanup to remove noise, then contour detection to find vehicle blobs.

**Tracking & counting** — blob centroids are tracked frame to frame; a virtual counting line increments the tally as a tracked vehicle crosses it, rendered live over the video with an on-screen counter.
**What it demonstrates** — real-time video processing and classical computer vision (no deep learning needed), with clean OpenCV / NumPy code.
**Stack** — Python, OpenCV and NumPy, in a Jupyter notebook plus a runnable script with sample footage.
**My role** — end-to-end: the detection and tracking pipeline and the counting overlay.
